9984.history: Baltic fleet. Lembit was commissioned in the Estonian Navy on 14 April 1937 but taken over by the Soviets on 19 August 1940. Lembit is preserved as a museum.

9985.history: Former British submarine L-55 (commissioned in Royal Navy on 19 December 1918) that was sunk on 4 June 1919. Raised by the Russians on 11 August 1928. Commissioned by the Russians on 10 December 1932. Used for training and as floating power station during the war. Stricken on 25 August 1950.

9986.built_by: Robert Jacob Inc. (City Island, New York, U.S.A.)
9986.history: Caught fire and sank off Halifax on 26 March 1941. 2 officers and 17 ratings perished.

9987.history: The vessel was requisitioned by the Royal Canadian Navy on 15 September 1939. HMCS Bras d'Or (T/A/Lt. Charles Avery Hornsby, RCNR) foundered in the Gulf of St. Lawrence on 19 October 1940. 5 officers and 25 ratings perished.
commanders: Charles Avery Hornsby, RCNR (26 Apr 1940 - 19 Oct 1940)

9988.built_by: Workman Clark & Co. Ltd. (Belfast, Northern Ireland)
9988.history: HMIS Pathan (Lt.Cdr. Karl Durston, RIN) was damaged on the 22nd by an internal explosion. Pathan sank the following day in position 18°56'N, 72°45'E.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Alfred Henry Watt, RIN (5 May 1939 - 20 Oct 1939)
Lt.Cdr. Ivan Bryan Warburton Heanly, RIN (20 Oct 1939 - mid 1940)

9989.history: Former German torpedo boat V 105 completed on 5 January 1915. Taken over by the Polish Navy after the First World War. Sunk by German Ju 87 (Stuka's) divebombers (IV/LG.1) in Oksywie harbour.
commanders: Kpt. mar. Tadeusz Rutkowski, ORP (??? - 1 Sep 1939)

9990.history: Jaskolka means swallow in English. Sunk at Jastarnia by German aircraft.
commanders: Kpt. mar. Tadeusz Borysiewicz, ORP (??? - 15 Sep 1939)

9991.history: Czajka means Peewit in English. Captured by the Germans in early October 1939. Returned to Poland in 1946. Scrapped on 31 December 1974.

9992.history: Rybitwa means tern in English. Sunk by German aircraft at Hel on 3 September 1939. Raised by the Germans. Returned to Poland in 1946. Stricken in 1972.

9993.history: Czapla means heron in English Sunk at Jastarnia by German aircraft.

9994.history: Zuraw means Grus in English. Captured by the Germans in early October 1939. Returned to Poland in 1946. Stricken on 31 December 1979.

9995.history: Mewa means seagull in English. Sunk at Hel by German aircraft on 3 September 1939. Raised by the Germans. Returned to Poland in 1946. Scrapped in May 1981.

9996.history: Sunk around 1600 hours on 3 September 1939 at Hela by German He59 aircraft (3./KüFl.Gr.506).
commanders: Kpt. mar. Stanislaw Artur Mieszkowski, ORP (??? - 3 Sep 1939)

9997.history: Scuttled at Hel on 30 September 1939. Raised by the Germans and renamed M 3109 / Heisternest. Sunk at Nantes, France by US bombers on 16 September 1943.

9998.history: Laid down as the Buckley class destroyer escort USS Yokes (DE 668). Redisignated APD 69 on 27 June 1944. Decommissioned 19 August 1946. Stricken 1 April 1964. Sold to be broken up for scrap in 1965.

9999.history: Laid down as the Buckley class destroyer escort USS Pavlic(DE 669). Redisignated APD 70 on 27 June 1944. Decommissioned 15 November 1946. Stricken 1 April 1967. Sold to be broken up for scrap on 1 July 1968.

10054.history: Reconstructed between 1931-35 and converted from coal to oil burning. She also received new turbogenerators, replacing the dynamos originally fitted. The oroginal 2 funnels were trunked into a single one. The antiaircraft armament was bolstered by the addition of 2-120 mm guns. She took no active part in the war since there was no need for her services by the time Brazil joined the Allies. Decommissioned on 20 September 1953 and sold for scrap.

10055.history: Sao Paulo was supposed to be reconstructed on the same lines as her sistership (i.e. conversion to oil burning and improvements to the electrical system etc) but a survey of her condition revealed that her hull and machinery were in too poor a condition to warrant the expenditure required to bring her up to standard and she spent the entire war laid up in Rio de Janeiro. Sold to be broken up for scrap in 1951. Sunk while under tow on 6 November 1951.

10056.history: On 4 July 1945, Bahia was stationed in the central Atlantic, halfway between Brazil and Africa, for potential lifesaving duties for US planes in trans-Atlantic transfer to locations in Europe & the Pacific. While conducting AA firings with her 20-mm guns against a kite streamed astern, a gunner shot the kite down and, following it on the way down, unintentionally hit a rack of depth charges on the fantail, which exploded, causing Bahia to sink within 3 minutes. The guns were not fitted with training stops to prevent them from shooting at angles where they endangered other weapon positions. The distater was not discovered until 8 July, when sistership Rio Grande do Sul arrived at the station to relieve Bahia, by which time less than half the crew was still alive, 294 had perished.

10064.history: Former British destroyer HMS Porpoise bought in 1920. Decommissioned on 13 September 1946.
